questions.question_text: A salesperson receives an earnest money check on a Friday afternoon. By when must the funds be deposited into the trust account?
distractors: By end of that business day
Within 5 calendar days
Before the offer is accepted
questions.explanation: California law requires trust funds to be deposited within 3 business days of receipt. This timeline starts when the agent receives the funds, not when the offer is accepted.

questions.question_text: A broker keeps $250 of personal funds in the trust account to cover bank fees and avoid closure. This practice is:
questions.correct_answer: Prohibited because personal funds may not exceed $200
distractors: Permitted since the amount covers legitimate bank fees
Permitted only with written consent of the current clients
Permitted if the broker maintains a separate ledger
questions.explanation: California allows up to $200 of personal broker funds in a trust account solely to keep it open and cover bank charges. The $250 here exceeds that limit and constitutes commingling.

questions.question_text: At a buyer's request, a broker deposits the buyer's earnest money into an interest-bearing trust account. Who may receive the interest earned?
questions.correct_answer: Only the owner of the funds — never the broker
distractors: The broker, if all parties consent in writing
The broker and the owner split the interest equally
The listing broker, as compensation for administering the account
questions.explanation: Under B&P Code §10145(d), a broker may place trust funds in an interest-bearing account only at the request of the funds' owner, with the account held in the broker's name as trustee for that beneficiary. Interest may never inure to the broker's benefit, directly or indirectly — even with the owner's written consent.

questions.question_text: A broker closes a sale and the commission is now earned. When may the broker withdraw the commission from the trust account?
questions.correct_answer: Only after the commission has been earned
distractors: Immediately upon deposit of the earnest money
Any time the account balance exceeds the broker's owed commissions
Only with written authorization from the seller at each withdrawal
questions.explanation: Brokers may withdraw commissions from the trust account only once the commission has been earned (i.e., upon close of escrow or as otherwise agreed). Early withdrawal constitutes commingling or conversion.

questions.question_text: A broker deposits client funds into a personal checking account labeled 'Real Estate Account.' Does this satisfy California trust account requirements?
questions.correct_answer: No, trust funds require an account designated as a trust account at a bank or recognized depository in California
distractors: Yes, any account labeled for real estate is sufficient
Yes, if the broker maintains a separate ledger
No, but only because the account is not at a federally insured institution
questions.explanation: B&P Code §10145 requires trust funds to be deposited into an account designated as a trust account, in the broker's name as trustee, at a bank or other recognized depository located in California. The bank's charter — state or federal — does not matter. A personal checking account does not qualify no matter how it is labeled.

questions.question_text: How long must a California broker retain transaction records such as contracts, listings, and disclosures?
questions.correct_answer: 3 years from closing or listing termination
distractors: 1 year from closing
2 years from closing
5 years from closing
questions.explanation: California requires brokers to retain transaction records for 3 years from the date of closing or from the termination of the listing, whichever applies.

questions.question_text: A DRE auditor arrives at a broker's office unannounced and requests access to all trust account records for the past two years. The broker refuses until given 48 hours' notice. The broker's refusal is:
questions.correct_answer: Unjustified because the DRE may audit without prior notice
distractors: Justified because 48 hours is a reasonable professional courtesy
Justified if the records are stored off-site
Unjustified only if the broker is under active investigation
questions.explanation: The DRE has authority to audit broker records at any time without advance notice. A broker who refuses or delays access is in violation of the Business and Professions Code.

questions.question_text: Which records must a California broker retain for audit purposes?
questions.correct_answer: Listings, contracts, trust records, correspondence, and disclosures
distractors: Contracts and trust records only
Trust account statements and escrow instructions only
Any records the broker deems relevant to past transactions
questions.explanation: California requires brokers to retain listings, purchase contracts, trust records, correspondence, and disclosure documents for three years. The requirement is comprehensive, not selective.

questions.question_text: A salesperson runs ads on Instagram using only the team name 'HomePros.' No broker name appears. These ads are:
questions.correct_answer: Non-compliant because all ads must prominently identify the broker
distractors: Compliant if the team name is registered with the DRE
Compliant for social media because platform character limits apply
Compliant if the salesperson's license number is included
questions.explanation: California advertising rules apply equally to all media including social media. All advertisements must prominently include the responsible broker's name. A team name alone without broker identification is a blind ad, which is prohibited.

questions.question_text: An unlicensed assistant working in a broker's office wants to show a listed home to prospective buyers while the licensed agent is out. The broker allows it. This arrangement is:
questions.correct_answer: Not permitted because showing property requires a real estate license
distractors: Permitted if the assistant has taken at least one real estate course
Permitted under direct broker supervision
Permitted for showings but not for open houses
questions.explanation: Unlicensed assistants may perform only clerical duties. Showing property to prospective buyers is a licensed activity. Allowing an unlicensed person to conduct showings exposes both the assistant and the broker to disciplinary action.

questions.question_text: A DRE audit finds a broker has no policies, procedures, or systems for reviewing transaction documents or overseeing salesperson activities. Why is the broker subject to discipline?
questions.correct_answer: Regulation 2725 requires supervision systems covering transactions, documents, trust funds, and advertising
distractors: Brokers must have policies but their content is not regulated
The broker is cited only if a client complaint was also filed
Supervision policies are optional for brokers with fewer than five agents
questions.explanation: Commissioner's Regulation 2725 defines reasonable supervision to include establishing policies, rules, procedures, and systems to review and manage licensed transactions, documents with a material effect on the parties, trust fund handling, advertising, and regular reports of salesperson activity — plus a system for monitoring compliance. A broker with no such systems has failed the supervision duty regardless of whether a client was harmed.

questions.question_text: A salesperson places an ad with the tagline 'Call Alex — the best deal in town!' and lists only a phone number. This ad is prohibited primarily because it is:
questions.correct_answer: A blind ad lacking the broker's name
distractors: Misleading about the agent's qualifications
Using unauthorized superlative language
Missing the salesperson's license number
questions.explanation: California prohibits blind ads, which are ads that do not identify the responsible broker. An ad with only the salesperson's name and a phone number — with no broker identification — is a blind ad.

questions.question_text: A broker is sued after a salesperson made fraudulent misrepresentations to a buyer during a showing. The broker had no knowledge of the salesperson's conduct. Under California law, which statement is most accurate?
questions.correct_answer: The broker may be liable because brokers are responsible for supervising salespersons
distractors: The broker has no liability because the fraud was the salesperson's act
The broker is liable only if the misrepresentation was in writing
The broker is liable only if the salesperson is judgment-proof
questions.explanation: California imposes supervisory responsibility on brokers for the acts of their salespersons. A broker who fails to properly supervise can be held liable even without direct knowledge of the salesperson's misconduct.

questions.question_text: A broker wants to open a trust account at a federally chartered bank headquartered in Nevada, which has a branch in Los Angeles. Does this satisfy California requirements?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, a bank authorized to do business in California qualifies
distractors: No, only California state-chartered banks qualify
No, the bank's headquarters must be in California
Yes, but only if the account is FDIC-insured
questions.explanation: California requires trust accounts at a bank or institution authorized to do business in the state. A federally chartered bank with a California branch meets this requirement because the key is authorization to operate in California, not state charter status.

questions.question_text: Which of the following tasks may a broker lawfully delegate to an unlicensed assistant?
questions.correct_answer: Scheduling showings and filing transaction paperwork
distractors: Hosting an open house and answering buyer questions
Preparing offer documents for the agent to review
Negotiating repair credits on behalf of the buyer
questions.explanation: Unlicensed assistants may perform clerical and administrative tasks such as scheduling and filing. They may not host open houses, answer substantive buyer questions, prepare offers, or negotiate — all of which require a license.
